speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number five, interview of Adam Boren, August 14, 1996-- Adam, when did you get in contact with the resistance organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: In about March, middle of March, in 1943, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I and two of-- friends of mine that we stuck together got in contact with-- or met with-- some of the fighters of the ZOB organization. They asked us they need-- that they need couriers between the-- the fighting group and the buildings. The-- the ZOB have broken through in cellars between buildings so that you could travel great part of the ghetto without going outside, just between buildings. Also, you could do it on a roof before they started bombing it and burning the houses.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we agreed, of course, to it. And what-- they said to just stay on the roof and watch what's happening. Are the Germans surrounding the walls? That means another action is coming. Is the Polish police-- is any concentration of the guards, Ukranian, or Latvians, or the-- of the SS German? And so that's how we got involved in it. But we were too young. And there was no weapons to give it to us, you know? There was such a scarcity. But the weapons and bullets that they wouldn't waste on amateurs or kids like us, you know? So they had the older ones. Now, besides giving information on German movements, did you do anything else for the resistance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we brought food where we could find it, in the bunkers, to them. We-- they asked us-- they needed bandages. They need disinfectants, iodine, and things for it. There was no medicine per se, but that was the basic things for wounded. We would carry that through.

speaker: interviewee
text: We would carry clothing for them, all the menial courier jobs. But mainly, we were watchdogs watching out continuously on a shifts. Are the Germans starting something in other area-- watching the gates, watching the walls. Are they assembling? Because they first started ringing up the ghetto with guards before an action. That was a sign that something gonna start. And where were you when the uprising in April begins?

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, I was asleep. One of the three of us had the guard duty. And he woke us up and said-- in the morning-- must've been, like, 6:00 in the morning. The light was just coming out-- that the ghetto is surrounded, that something gonna start. And we, you know pass on. We run to the-- to the group, which was a couple streets away, a couple blocks away, and told them there is something happening. There is a assembling around the ghetto. This looks like a action going to start. We see a lot of military men, soldiers, Ukrainians, because they wore different uniforms, brown uniforms for the Latvians, black uniforms for the Ukrainians, and green uniforms for the SS. So we says there are a lot of them-- and Polish police in navy uniforms. Something going to start. And they already, by the way, knew it. Another courier group told them, also, that from other parts, that this happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: What street was the group that you were a courier for located on in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were on-- on Niska Street-- that's N-I-S-K-A-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] right near-- right next to the Umschlagplatz, which was the border of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the beginning of the fighting on August 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the fighting that-- we actually saw some of it, you know? Actually, it was, you know, we making glorious fighting. It was such a uneven thing. The Germans expected, from the February experience, that something will happen. And I'm sure that they had spies in the ghetto, you know, collaborators that must have given information. They moved in with the tanks. The first tanks were hit with Molotov cocktails and with-- with grenades. And all we saw is fire coming out. We didn't see the tanks. They were blocked by-- by buildings. There were-- also, we heard machine gun fire from the ghetto. By the way, that's only one machine gun. That's all we had, one machine gun. If [? wachta ?] ever talked to you, the Polish Underground, the AK, Armia Krajowa, the National Polish Underground Army, which was well-equipped with weapons left from the war that they hid Polish weapons from the-- from the war would not give or sell the weapons to the Jews. They say it's waste. The weapons were bought on a black market. And a Leftist underground Polish called AL, Armia Ludowa, People's Army, sold and gave some hand weapons, carbines, and pistols, and a few grenades. So there was very little of-- of defense, mostly molotov, mostly bombs made in the ghetto. Because they smuggled in chemicals, phosphor and others, and made bombs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever help to make any weapons?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I had no input to it at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's how it started. And it was for no more than a week, maybe eight days, where we acted as couriers. And then, the group that we were working with moved out, further out. We couldn't be in touch with them, because the Germans were burning building by building, and attacking it, and gaining control. But the fighting was going on continuously. There was weapons that we captured from Germans that were killed. Also, there were factories making uniform for the German army. Some of those uniforms were stolen, and the-- and the fighters put on the uniforms and got down to the-- down to the streets, shot some Germans, got their weapons. So the fighting was going on in a small way. Groups such as the brushworks, a factory that make brushes and others, had barricaded themself. And they were fighting with the-- with the Germans. And of course, you're fighting with a fully-equipped army. The air force, the-- the Luftwaffe, was bombing the ghetto with incendiary bombs. With the dive bomb, it's very precise. So nothing on the Aryan sides was hit. The Germans were, with flame throwers, burning house after house and blowing it up. People-- like, me were hidden in the bunkers, which were cellars, blocked off cellars. They were finding it with a listening device. And they had sensitive listening devices that they were walking and listening for voices of movement. We were in one of the buildings, I and my-- by the way, in one of the trips that I had taken with a fellow to deliver some clothing to the group, when I returned, I found my-- my wife-- my mother was shot, killed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We, the three of us, we went from one bunker-- and it was destroyed-- to another. In one of the bunkers, we was there where the German have put to fire above. And they threw grenades. And the ceiling collapsed on us with the fire. And I got burned. I got third-degree burn on my hand, left hand, and my right foot. My skin peeled off like a glove. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was in horrible pain, of course. We ran out from there. My friends carried me, actually, to another bunker. And I just had a-- a shirt, old shirt that I wrapped around. I had-- they started infection. It was a couple days. Got green. I got fever. And then, one morning, the German had discovered us. They threw gas grenades, smoke grenades into it. And we had to get out. And we were in the building's garden, so to say. A friend of mine gave me a cigarette. There was an SS man, a young boy-- he must have been 18-- lighting a cigarette. And I walked over and pointed out. And another SS man right next to him hit me with the-- with the butt of cigarette, knocked the cigarette out of my head, and knocked me off floor. Never lit a cigarette. They took us to Umschlagplatz. Umschlagplatz had a building there. The-- the room was full of-- of the inhabitants. Some were wounded. Some were very sick because they threw those smoke grenades or gas grenades, were vomiting, were laying there, green in their face. We were there two days-- no food, no water, just packed. I was half dead. I was in high fever. I-- I had terrible infection in the hand and the foot, couldn't walk. They brought us down and put us in a railroad cars, closed railroad cars, packed. And truly, I don't give up easy. But I just didn't have any-- any strength. I could have-- if I was in a whole thing, I could escape from there. It basically was a window you could have broke. People did that and escaped. Some did not successfully, because the SS was riding on a roof. And anybody escaping, that would shoot at. But some escaped. Anyway, they brought us to Majdanek concentration camp. Majdanek is in a suburb of Lublin, the city of Lublin. That's a eastern city. That's southeast of Warsaw. It was only a few hours' train ride, I think four or five hours' train ride. Right When we got to Majdanek, they-- on a run, they opened the-- the cars, and there were people dead already. And on a run-- again, screaming and beating-- they run us to barracks in Majdanek. And we had to undress naked. And there was a whole line of prisons. There were a few thousands of the prisoners in the transport. Inside the barrack was a bench. There were several prisoner barbers sitting at the bench. And naked, you had to pass them. They would, with clippers, shave or clip your hair and your pubic hair. And then you go further, and there was an SS officer standing, looking at you. And with a whip, he would direct you to the left or to the right. There were two doors. And of course, later found out that one door was gas chamber, and one door was to the-- to the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got in front of the barber, he asked me, what do you got here? I had my hand and foot in a-- in a-- in the rag, wrapped around. And I opened up and showed him it was old, stinking pus. And he says, stay with me. Don't move. And while other prisoners kept going, I stayed with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: The officer finally left the post, went outside to see how many-- possibly. I didn't know what's his mind-- but walk outside to look how many prisoners are still there. And the barber told me, go through the right door, or run to the right door. And I went to the right door, which was the concentration camp. And he saved my life. And I don't know who he was. I never met him again. But had I gone through with my hand and foot, I would have been going out the chimney. In the camp, we were put into a tent. I was sick. There was a Polish doctor in a-- in the barrack, not-- not working for the hospital or anything, who had with him-- he looked at my stuff, the infection that it was. And he had with him a disinfectant, a crystals called [? calihypamine ?] [? cancum-- ?] hypermangan of potash. They're sanitary crystal. Use them for disinfection-- totally disinfect. They blue. They dissolve into a blue color, the disinfectant. And he gave me some. And he said, dissolve them in water and bathe you hand and foot. Put some rags, wet rags, around that with it. And within few days, the-- the infection disappeared, and a scab formed. But we had to go and work, dig-- dig ditches and carry stones on a run. And I hid few times behind barracks, because I couldn't carry anything. But in a few days, the scab fell off, and the new skin grow. It helps if you are very young. If you're very young, both your body and your mind recuperate very fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in Majdanek, were you with anyone that you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your friends with you, from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody. Ok.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't know what happened to the two friends that were with me. They-- they were in Umschlagplatz, but they may have been put in another car. Or they may have been shot. I have no idea. I never saw them again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were-- was the food supply like in Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Majdanek was a horrible camp. It was destruction camp. You work on a run with the prisoner kapos beating you. You pick up stone rocks, run with them several hundred yards, deposit them. Another group will pick up those same rocks and bring them back or put them someone else, constantly on a run. The food was a thin slice of bread in the morning and a soup, thin soup, in the evening with a-- there was, like-- like a beet type that you feed the cattle. It's a big beet, white beet. Forgot the name of it. This is-- was a standard fare. But the terror was horrendous, not only from the SS, but from the prisoners. On the block that I was in, on field four-- that was a men's field, field four in Majdanek-- my-- my block leader was a Warsaw Jew, a porter. He was a porter before the war. 'Porter' means carrying bags. He was a sadistic bastard, beating prisoners, big guy. I don't know if he got their food portions. There was another one like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the names?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to call him Moishe Gazal-- [? 'gazal' ?] means 'devil'-- or Herr Moishe de [? Gazal. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he ever do anything to physically harm you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I got beaten by him. But worse than that, I-- one time, the kommando-- we had to straighten out the stones, on our knees, between the wires, between the fences in the camp. There were two fences, electrified fence, and then there was a-- a very fine stone, and another fence. And we had on our knees. And the commander of the camp, Kramer, Obersturmfuhrer Kramer-- 'Krah-mer'-- K-R-A-M-E-R-- was riding his horse along the-- along the wires, along the fences. And he didn't like-- or somebody fell down. And he ordered punishment, beating. They had placed a-- a special bench they had for beating, for whipping, where you lay down. They strip your pants off, exposing your buttock. And two prisoners with whips would beat you.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were 25 whips. I've passed out about 10. I mean. They cut up my-- my buttock. I have infection. You want to see it? I still have holes. I'll show you. [PAUSES FOR 29 SECONDS] But I recuperated. I wouldn't last-- I wouldn't last very long in Majdanek. They announced that they looking for electricians. So I went to the barrack, I registered, and I said my grandfather was electrician. My father was electrician. I am electrician helper. I know electrical, even though all I knew about electrical, it's in a class, how to wire a bell from a battery, you know? I figured that anything would be better what I am.

speaker: interviewee
text: They put us in a separate barrack. They took me on. And a couple days later, they shipped us in cattle cars, but not packed, with a SS man in each car, to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was in [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] July or August. I was only two months in Majdanek, that's all, I think. In fact, in that book that I just showed you, there's exact date that I arrive. I don't remember [LAUGHS] the date, but that is an exact date.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived in Auschwitz, main Auschwitz. Auschwitz consisted of many camps. The main camp Auschwitz was a foreign Polish military-- what they call it? [GERMAN] in German-- a barracks. Then there was Birkenau, Auschwitz, which was a concentration camp, and the gas chambers, and the crematoria. There were several small camps attached to Auschwitz, one of them being Buna-- 'Buna' in German, means 'synthetic rubber'-- which was run by IG Farben industry. Supposedly IG Farben industry is the one that, what I learned later, induced the SS to build Auschwitz for the production of military supplies such as powders, and weapons, and synthetic rubber, and synthetic oil. There were also coal mines, because that was all inside Silesia, which was the coal mine district [INAUDIBLE] in Poland, also attached to Auschwitz. So there were many camps. The camp, my camp, that-- where they sent me to Buna was called Auschwitz II.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, let's just go back from before you were sent to Auschwitz II. When you first arrived at Auschwitz, what do you remember of your first day there?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we-- yes, when we arrived at Auschwitz again, we had to undress. And we went through a selecktia, they call it-- selection-- where the SS officer, whoever he was, looked at you. You had to run past him and back again naked. And again, either you went-- he made a move to the right or to the left on a field, which, one means you were accepted, and the other, you went for recuperation, as they state in their book, which was the gas chamber. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I passed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, he looked at me at my buttock, this-- the thing I had it. And he said, what's that? Oh, and I said-- I lied, and I said-- oh, I had it many, many years. And he just let me go. We then were set up in a single row-- there were a few hundreds of us-- and given pieces of paper with a number on it. And prisoners came with the tattoo needles and tattooed the number.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Could we-- could just say the number and then show it to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: The number that I-- that was tattooed on me is 127956, or in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. That was my name, my address, my identity. And this, on the left forearm--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- it was my total identity. In fact, any time-- time you reported, you report haftling, which, in German, means 'prisoner'-- haftling [GERMAN]-- prisoner 127956, reporting. That was my number.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you sent after you were tattooed?

speaker: interviewee
text: After we were tattooed, we were given prison uniforms, new ones-- or not new ones, but new from the camp, from Auschwitz-- stripe uniforms with a printed tag showing the number, which we had to sew on on the left breast of the jacket. And on the upper part of the pants, the 127956 was, on the pants, sewn here and sewn across the left breast with identification. And the identification was a Star of David with a one triangle being red and another triangle yellow, the two triangles forming the Star of David. The other prisoners that I met later that had, for instance, green triangle down were criminals. Black triangle up were antisocial. Pink triangle up were homosexual. So they identify-- and red triangle down were political. They identify prisoners of the category. The Jews have one category, the Star of David of the two colors.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you given for your feet?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you given for your feet?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the feet, we had wooden shoes, originally, that we came back from Majdanek. They were shoes that the Dutch wear. Later, we were issued shoes from the people that they killed--

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: --that you-- not-- not didn't fit you, but you exchanged with other prisoners, that you found one that more or less fits you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your food and living quarters in Buna compare with Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were brought by trucks to Buna and put into a quarantine tent. That means, for four weeks, we were in the tent. And the work was very hard. We had to drag electric-- heavy electric cables that you sometimes see, those big, giant wheels with cables on it, lay them out in ditches, in mud and things. We got a, I would say, piece of bread that would be quarter pound, a little more than quarter pound and, twice a day, soup, a soup in the lunch time and a soup at night-- very thin soup, watery soup, no potatoes. That was the-- the-- the food, but there was no persecution. There was-- they were-- left us alone, more or less. After four weeks, I was assigned to an electric kommando. Buna was a camp attached to IG Farben factory. IG Farben was the world's largest chemical factory-- in fact, associated with American Exxon-- today Exxon, but the old Esso. They build a-- the prisoners built it-- a factory producing synthetic rubber and synthetic oil. I was attached to the kommando number 141. My kapo was a German Jew from Hamburg, a young boy who already spent several years in concentration camp. His name is Leon-- Leo Ziegler--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Z-I-E-G-L-E-R-- very nice, very kind guy. Only became kapo because of his long term in prison. I mean, he was-- and he spoke fluent German. Of course, he was German. The kommando was attached to a power station, high voltage power, to build control cells controlling the switching of the electrical power to the different processing plants that they had. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My job was to wire those from drawings, from schematics. Being sufficiently intelligent, I could read the schematics. And watching others, I very fast picked up the wiring of it. The nice things about it was that while you work, the SS, who was parading back and forth, would not touch you. Because the-- your bosses were German mechanics, German foremen who are responsible for the wiring. But if you did something that was-- they considered sabotage, you were executed in camp. After you came to camp, they kill you, because you committed sabotage. So you'd be very careful. And did a-- a decent job, they left you alone. The master that I had, the German master, his name was Miller. He was a decent man-- at least he looked like. In fact, I was sick once there. I, again, had temperature, and he saw it. And there were cells with doors with high voltage. There were high voltage rails running the whole ceilings, like 10,000, 20,000 volts.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on that door was a dead head with two bones and 'Warning-- do not open,' you know, 'dangerous.' He used to open the cell, and tell me, lay down on the bottom, and close the cell. And the SS would never get near it, OK? [LAUGHS] So he let me rest.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I worked. And I-- and in compared to Majdanek was incomparable. Because for once, we had-- even though the banks-- lost my [INAUDIBLE]-- the bunks in the-- in the barracks were three bunks. You had individual bunk for yourself. You were-- you got the, three times, food a day. And also, we smuggled. We smuggled things from the camp to the ward. There were Polish workers, civilian workers.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
